content: Yes, i've updated the title of this thread to specifically say "for strict monoidal 2-categories".

This all started over here, where I was posting about "Hopf monoids in species", and using these higher string diagrams to make sense out of all this. Since then I've made a lot of progress, and drawn many more of these diagrams. It's all been very helpful to me in understanding how all the moving parts connect.

And I'm not focusing on arbitrary monoidal 2-categories, but one very important example, the cartesian monoidal 2-category of 1-categories. This is where monoidal categories live! For example, drawing these diagrams makes clear (to me) that monoidal functors are not just functors preserving some structure but there's additional structure on the functor that tells you how this preservation happens. This is the (co-)lax monoidal structure on a functor.

I may have gotten confused interpreting the "strictness" of these diagrams as some kind of stabilization, which is why i was wondering about the periodic table over here. I'm still wondering about this: is "strictness" (morphisms becoming equations) related to "stabilization" (co-dimension of diagrams) ? It's always hard to give an answer to "is this related?" questions but maybe this one has an obvious answer.

Stabilization has essentially nothing to do with isomorphisms becoming equations. It's more related to isomorphisms obeying more equations. For example, when we stabilize and go from braided to symmetric monoidal categories, the braiding isomorphism does not become an equation; instead, it obeys a new equation.

But when I say "equation" here, that's because in a category, it's not too strict to say 1-morphisms are isomorphisms, and obey equations. If we were in a higher category we'd have to adjust what I said. For example, when we stabilize once and go from braided to sylleptic monoidal bicategories, the braiding equivalence gets equipped with a new iso-2-morphism, the "syllepsis". Then when we stabilize again and go from sylleptic to symmetric monoidal bicategories, the syllepsis obeys a new equation. But nothing is ever strict, i.e. evil.

What's true is that in the strict world stabilization happens in just two steps! You get strict n-categories, strict monoidal n-categories, and then strict commutative monoidal n-categories. That's it.

Ok, so I'm going to think of these "strict" higher string diagrams as living in three dimensions. I don't need any more dimensions to explain the "strictness", those are just extra equations that the calculus obeys.

I am still a bit confused though, because the cartesian monoidal product here is symmetric, so maybe i do need more dimensions, but just not for "strictness" purposes... This must be related to your comment about "two step" stabilization...

For example, here is a swap of 0-cells
image.png
I think this diagram "lives in" 4d because i've drawn it with braid decorations to show that one surface is going in front of the other in 4d.

And I would draw this to denote a swap in a stable diagram, "living in" 5d i think:
image.png
So from your "two step" comment, it looks like I just take the stable diagram because we can't ever see the non-stable case when things are strict. This is very helpful, thanks !

I'm not sure why you mentioned cartesian monoidal structures, but you're making me want to mention: very few cartesian monoidal categories are equivalent to commutative monoidal categories. For example, if 2 is the 2-element set, the symmetry

B2,2:2×2→2×2 B_{2,2}: 2 \times 2 \to 2 \times 2B2,2​:2×2→2×2

is not the identity, so (FinSet,×)(\textsf{FinSet}, \times)(FinSet,×) is not equivalent to a commutative monoidal category.

I am very confused by your picture of a "swap of 0-cells", because you're drawing a 2-dimensional surface instead of the usual 1-dimensional strings we use for this 1-morphism.

Yes, these are diagrams for the cartesian monoidal 2-category of 1-categories. So these sheets are categories (see Dan Marsden's paper), which are 0-cells, and the swap C*D --> D*C is a functor, a 1-cell, that swaps the sheets.
Okay, I don't know this stuff. Why is it good to draw categories (0-cells) as sheets instead of as something 0-dimensional?
It sounds like you're secretly treating them as higher-dimensional cells. I can imagine various reasons for doing that... but why?

Arrow diagrams (globular diagrams) have the objects as zero dimensional, 1-morphisms are 1d and 2-morphisms are 2-d, etc., and string diagrams dualize this: objects are now 2d, 1-morphisms are 1d and 2-morphisms are 0d.
So a category here is 2d, a functor is a 1d line sepearating regions, and a natural transform is a 0d point separating two lines.
Oh, okay, that business! Duh!

Right, i probably should have gone over this above...
So categories are regions, like this:
image.png

Functors look like a line separating regions:
image.png

And natural transforms are 0-dimensional "pips"
image.png

(it's kind of funny how there's no preferred direction in what i drew so far...)
And cartesian product goes into the third dimension, by stacking regions
image.png

And then a monoidal category is this collection of data:
image.png
I write these diagrams in the algebraic direction, right-to-left, bottom-up. And also there's a bunch of equations satisfied by the data..
